content:
Key Findings

  • Chorley Borough Council's published spending data reveals a total expenditure of £372.2M.
  • This substantial sum was disbursed across 21,421 individual transactions.
  • The analysed data covers a five-year period, spanning financial years 2021/22 through to 2025/26.
  • The scale of this spending highlights the critical need for robust procurement intelligence and public oversight.

Understanding Chorley's Spending Landscape

Chorley Borough Council, a key district authority in Lancashire, manages significant public funds to deliver essential services to its residents. An analysis of its published spending records, covering five financial years from 2021/22 to 2025/26, reveals a total expenditure of £372.2M. This considerable sum represents the council's financial commitment to various projects, services, and suppliers over half a decade. The sheer volume of this spending underscores the importance of transparency and detailed scrutiny in local government finance.

The council's spending is broken down into 21,421 individual transactions. Each of these transactions, whether large or small, contributes to the overall financial picture of the borough. Understanding how this £372.2M is allocated across thousands of payments is vital for ensuring public money is spent efficiently and effectively. Public spending platforms like AI DOGE aim to make this complex financial data accessible, allowing citizens to gain insight into the operational costs and investment priorities of their local council.

The Importance of Procurement Intelligence

Procurement intelligence involves the systematic analysis of an organisation's purchasing activities to identify patterns, efficiencies, and potential areas for improvement. For Chorley Borough Council, with its £372.2M expenditure over five years, such intelligence is crucial. Analysing the 21,421 transactions can help identify key trends in supplier relationships, contract durations, and the distribution of funds across different departments. Without detailed analysis, it can be challenging to assess whether the council is consistently achieving value for money in its procurement processes.

Specific patterns that procurement intelligence seeks to uncover include price escalation, where the cost of goods or services increases significantly over time or across similar contracts. Another area of focus is cross-department splitting, where larger contracts might be divided into smaller transactions across different departments, potentially to avoid higher-level scrutiny or competitive tendering thresholds. Contract concentration, which examines how much of the £372.2M is directed towards a limited number of suppliers, is also a key aspect. While specific data on these patterns for Chorley is not detailed here, the existence of 21,421 transactions provides a rich dataset for such future analysis, highlighting the potential for deeper understanding of the council's procurement strategies.

Ensuring Oversight and Value for Money

The scale of Chorley Borough Council's spending, amounting to £372.2M over five years, demands robust oversight mechanisms. Every pound spent by the council is public money, collected from local taxpayers. Therefore, ensuring that this money is spent wisely and achieves the best possible outcomes for the community is a fundamental responsibility. Effective procurement practices are central to this, aiming to secure high-quality services and goods at competitive prices. Public platforms play a role in facilitating this oversight by making the raw data, such as the 21,421 transactions, available for examination.

Public scrutiny of spending data helps to reinforce accountability and can drive improvements in procurement processes. When citizens and journalists can easily access and analyse how the £372.2M is being spent, it creates an environment where councils are encouraged to maintain high standards of financial management. The ability to explore the details of each of the 21,421 transactions empowers the public to ask informed questions and hold their elected representatives and council officers accountable for their financial decisions, ultimately contributing to better governance and greater value for public money.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to Chorley Borough Council about specific procurement patterns, such as contract durations or supplier selection criteria.
  • Attend council committee meetings, which are open to the public, to hear discussions on financial decisions and procurement strategies.
  • Check the council's published contracts register for details on awarded contracts and their values.
  • Write to your local councillor about concerns regarding the transparency or efficiency of the council's spending of the £372.2M.
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data for Chorley Borough Council and other Lancashire councils yourself.
This article is based on Chorley Borough Council's published spending data covering 2021/22 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
